coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin sub-3 nm nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot layers coated TiO2 nanocomposites as high-performance photocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Sejung
Chang, Haeun
Choi, Jae-Young
Song, Youngjun
Heller, Michael J.
coredata.dc:description: We introduce a facile fabrication process for ultrathin nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD) layers on TiO2 nanoparticles in order to develop high-performance photocatalysts. NGQDs are grown either by graphitization of precursors or direct deposition of premade NGQDs. Photocatalytic performance is shown to be dependent on the thickness of NGQD layers and doping levels of C and N atoms on TiO2 surfaces. The nanocomposites shown to absorb a broad range of visible light and narrow the bandgap by 0.38 eV, resulting in the increased density of photoinduced eletron-hole pairs and efficient charge separation at the interface between the NGQDs and TiO2.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NGQDs and NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University of California, San Diego Heller Lab Royalty Fund BNGTTLB

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by University of California, San Diego Heller Lab Royalty Fund BNGTTLB. The authors are grateful for Dr. Jennifer Wright for fruitful discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication procedure for production of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic process for NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites by either direct growth of NGQDs or direct deposition of premade NGQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. (b) TEM image of an NT–I nanocomposite by direct growth of NGQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles, resulting in a less than 1 nm thickness of NGQDs layers. (c) TEM image of an NT–II nanocomposite by direct deposition of premade NGQDs onto the TiO2 surface, resulting in a 2.8 nm thickness of NGQDs layers. Scale bar: 10 nm.

Chemical composition analysis of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites by XPS. (a) and (b) represent the high resolution of N 1 s XPS peaks to show the pyridine- and pyrrolic-like N bond formation. (c) High resolution of Ti 2p XPS analysis of NT–I, NT–II nanocomposites, and bare TiO2 to represent the peak shift after formation of composites with NGQDs.

Optical properties and photoactive performance of NT nanocomposites. (a) UV–visible spectra of NT–I, NT–II, and bare TiO2 to determine light absorption properties. (b) Tauc plots to show the relationship between [αhν]2 and the photon energy to determine the bandgap of NT nanocomposites. The bandgap is 3.18, 2.95, and 2.80 eV for the bare TiO2, NT–II, and NT–I nanocomposites, respectively. (c) Time profile of ST absorbance spectrum under light irradiation for 90 min. (d) Photodegradation of ST under light irradiation by bare TiO2, NGQDs (control), and NT nanocomposites.
Schematic illustration of the possible photogenerated electrons flow mechanism of NT nanocomposites under light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin sub-3 nm nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot layers coated TiO2 nanocomposites as high-performance photocatalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoengineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano-Bioengineering, Incheon National University, 119 Academy-ro, Incheon 22012, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Nanoengineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA (M. J. Heller).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We introduce a facile fabrication process for ultrathin nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD) layers on TiO2 nanoparticles in order to develop high-performance photocatalysts. NGQDs are grown either by graphitization of precursors or direct deposition of premade NGQDs. Photocatalytic performance is shown to be dependent on the thickness of NGQD layers and doping levels of C and N atoms on TiO2 surfaces. The nanocomposites shown to absorb a broad range of visible light and narrow the bandgap by 0.38 eV, resulting in the increased density of photoinduced eletron-hole pairs and efficient charge separation at the interface between the NGQDs and TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: As a green technology, utilization of light-driven technologies can be widely used for elimination of pollution, water splitting, and photoelectrochemical processes to meet energy demands [1–5]. It is required to ensure an efficient generation of photogenerated charge carriers, separation of electron-hole pairs, and efficient transfer of charge carriers for the development of photoactive materials [6–9]. Due to the quantum confinement effect of nanomaterials, photocatalytic performance can be regulated by surface states, geometries, and the degree of crystallinity at a nanoscale for efficient light harvesting [10–13]. While TiO2 nanostructures are widely used in development of high-performance of photocatalysts, they have been limited to the absorption of narrow wavelengths and mismatch in energy levels for practical uses [6,7,11,13]. In order to overcome the limitations, the heterojunction structures composed of graphitic carbon nanostructures and semiconductor nanomaterials have been developed by interfacial engineering at nanoscale levels to achieve the alignment of energy levels, which facilitates fine tuning of structure, composition, bandgap, and surface reaction site [3,8,9,14–16]. Herein, we exploit the effect of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) on rutile TiO2 nanoparticles on photocatalytic performance: (1) Regulation of the thickness of NGQD layers and density of doping with carbon and nitrogen atoms on TiO2, and (2) the subsequent absorption of light in a broad range and photocatalytic performance. Our study of designing these nanocomposite-based photocatalysts will complement the catalytic performance we currently see in a desired nanoscale regime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NGQDs and NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The premade nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared through hydrothermal methods (4 h at 160 °C) using 1 mmol of citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥ 99.5%) and 3 mmol of urea (Fisher Chemicals, 99.5%) in 10 ml of deionized (DI) water (SI, Fig. S1). Then, the final products were purified by using a dialysis bag for 24 h. In order to fabricate the NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites, bare 0.1 g of TiO2 nanoparticles (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.995%) were first dispersed for two hours by ultrasonication in 10 ml of DI water with citric acid (1 mmol) for direct growth (NT–I) or glycine (3 mmol) for deposition of premade NGQDs onto TiO2 nanoparticles (NT–II). For the NT–I nanocomposites, 3 mmol of urea was added to the solution containing TiO2 nanoparticles with citric acid and ultrasonication (Fisherbrand™, 110 W) continued for 1 h. The particles were then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave followed and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. The final product of NT–I nanocomposites was purified by using a dialysis bag and centrifugation. The NT–II nanocomposites were prepared by direct deposition of premade NGQDs onto surface-mediated TiO2 nanoparticles with glycine by vigorous stirring for 2 h. Then, the mixture was isolated by centrifugation at 5000 rpm and washed with DI water.

Photocatalytic performance was measured by investigating degradation capability of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites under light irradiation, wherein the solution includes 1 ml of Safranin T (ST, 1 mM). The photo-induced degradation was performed by a UV lamp (1.3 W cm−2) at a wavelength of 324 nm. The performance of photocatalytic activities was determined by measuring the absorbance of ST at a wavelength of 520 nm every 10 min for 90 min.

The morphologies of the NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites were observed by scanning electron microscopy (ESEM, Hitach) at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on an AXIS Supra (Kratos) photoelectron spectrometer for the analysis of C 1s, N 1s, and Ti 2p peaks. The UV–vis spectra were studied using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer to investigate the absorption properties of the bare TiO2, NGQDs-TiO2 nanocomposites and ST.

The TiO2 nanocomposites with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been prepared by the following routes: (1) direct growth of NGQDs using nitrogen-including precursors onto TiO2 surfaces via a hydrothermal process (NT–I), and (2) direct deposition of premade NGQDs onto a TiO2 surface (NT–II) (Fig. 1 a). The NT–I nanocomposites are prepared by employing citric acid (CA) and urea to create NGQDs via a hydrothermal process, while the NT–II nanocomposites are prepared by using glycine to produce positively charged TiO2 nanoparticles followed by co-assembly of premade NGQDs. TEM images (Fig. 1b and c) show the TiO2 nanocomposites coated with ultrathin layers of NGQDs; the NT–I nanocomposites have less than 1 nm of NGQD layers, and the NT–II nanocomposites show the NGQD layers with ∼ 2.8 nm of thickness.

The well-defined crystal structure of TiO2 nanoparticles is shown to have 0.333 nm of lattice spacing, implying [1 0 1] crystal planes of rutile TiO2 phase (Fig. S1) [17]. According to TEM analysis, the thicknesses of coated NGQD layers are determined by 1.2 ± 0.43 nm and 2.9 ± 0.28 nm for NT–I and NT–II nanocomposites, respectively (Fig. S2). Compared to bare TiO2, the surfaces of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites are not changed, wherein those morphological features can be compared to the nanocomposites coated with graphene or graphene oxide, resulting in the irregular wrinkled and interconnected coverage of the surface [3]. We assumed that the NGQDs are finely deposited into the surface of the individual TiO2 nanoparticles. To verify the effect of fabrication process by assisting the surface modification in the hybrid nanocomposites, we deposited premade NGQDs on TiO2. The resulting thickness of NGQDs on the TiO2 nanoparticle surface is ∼ 7.2 ± 1.18 nm, and the deposition is irregular (Fig. S3). Therefore, the surface-mediated TiO2 nanoparticles lead to well-grown sub-3 nm NGQDs layers on the TiO2 surface.

The chemical composition of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites are analyzed by using X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum shows the distinct peaks for C 1 s at 284.6 eV, N 1 s at 400 eV, O 1 s at 529.5 eV, and Ti 2p3/2 at 458.3 eV (Fig. S4). In order to analyze the relative elemental contents and bonding types of N 1 s and Ti 2p3/2, the analysis of high resolution of XPS spectrums were carried out, as shown in Fig. 2 . The relative nitrogen contents of the nanocomposites are 4.23% and 7.89% for NT–I and NT–II, respectively, indicating a higher proportion of nitrogen atoms for the thicker NGQD layers of NT–II nanocomposites. Surface modification of TiO2 is sufficiently accomplished by using either glycine or citric acid because of the affinity of the TiO2 surface to hydroxyl groups. The deconvolution of the high resolution of N 1 s peaks for NT–I and NT–II show two different types of binding formation; pyridine- (399.6 eV) and pyrrolic-nitrogen (400.5 eV) in the TiO2 lattice. The two different types of N bonding formation indicate the presence of graphitic structures and oxidized states of the N atom [18]. In addition, the pyrrolic-nitrogen structures in NGQDs can be identified as chemisorbed on the TiO2 surfaces. XPS analysis of N 1s peak shows that nitrogen atoms can form the NOTi with a rutile TiO2 crystalline phase and nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon structure at an interface between NGQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles during the introduction of nitrogen sources to the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The high resolution analysis of C 1s peaks is also investigated to show the bonding formation of COTi (Fig. S4). The high resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 are observed at 458.4 and 464.2 eV, respectively, wherein the peak positions indicate the presence of Ti (IV) in the bare TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 2c) [19]. However, the binding energy of Ti is slightly reduced by 0.2–0.3 eV after coating with NGQD layers for NT–I and II nanocomposites, indicating the successful doping of C and N atoms in the TiO2 lattice structures [20]. Fig. 3 a demonstrates optical properties by measuring UV–visible absorption, wherein the absorption edge and maximum band is red-shifted for both types of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites. The Tauc plots for the relationship of [αhν]2 to photon energy show the relationship corresponding to photon energy for each spectrum, resulting in a bandgap of bare rutile TiO2 and both NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites (Fig. 3b). The bandgap of bare rutile TiO2 is 3.18 eV, which is similar to the reported value (3.20 eV), and the of NT–II is reduced to 2.95 eV [9,17]. Instead, the bandgap of NT–I nanocomposite is substantially reduced to 2.80 eV. The narrowing bandgap is due to the doping effect of Ti with C and N atoms on the TiO2 surface by forming well-defined NGQD layers to TiO2. The doping effect leads to the rearrangement of Ti atom crystalline structures. During the surface treatment of TiO2, Ti atoms on the surface can form bonds with C atoms such as Ti–O–C, which prevents π-electrons of NGQDs can easily bind with Ti atoms during the graphitization on the TiO2 surface without phase transformation. In a similar process of the doping effect, the band energy level is induced by change of conduction band, which results from less interaction between unpaired π-electrons and Ti atoms followed by a red-shift of band-edge and reduction of bandgap of NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites [3–5,7,13].

On the basis of the above results, the photocatalytic performance of nanocomposites is investigated by monitoring the photodegradation of organic dyes. The absorption spectrum of organic dyes according to the time profile is shown in Fig. 4 a; the absorption band at 520 nm is associated with organic dyes (Safranin T, ST). The reduced absorption band at 520 nm indicates the decomposition of ST with increasing irradiation time. The decomposition rates of STs are evaluated by measuring the change of absorption band at 520 nm. In Fig. 4b, while the bare rutile TiO2 nanoparticles lead to degradation with a rate constant, k = 1.86 × 10−3 min−1, the degradation of STs are significantly increased with a rate constant, k = 5.00 × 10−3 min−1, which shows that the well-defined deposited NGQDs on TiO2 highly enhance the photocatalytic performance. This is attributed to the fact that the photocatalytic performance of nanocomposites is enhanced due to the bandgap alignment of the nanocomposites, which results in efficient visible light absorption. The formation of Ti–O–C and Ti–O–N bonds are also associated with efficient photogenerated charge transfer at the interface from excited organic dyes to TiO2 nanoparticles through a conduction pathway in the graphitic carbon nanostructures (Fig. 4). According to the thickness of NGQD layers on TiO2 nanoparticles, the photodegradation activities by NT–I and II nanocomposites are largely affected by the thicknesses of graphitic carbon layers, resulting in enhanced photodegradation kinetics by 2.7 times at ∼1 nm thickness of NGQD layers [21]. Therefore, we concluded that the mono- or bilayers of NGQDs on TiO2 have a large photodegradation kinetic due to the enhanced decomposition rate based on the highly sufficient photogenerated charge transfer as well as photo-induced charge generation and splitting electron-hole pairs.

In principle, the higher energy level of rutile TiO2 than that of graphene blocks to transfer of photogenerated electron from organic dyes physically absorbed on NGQDs to the surface of TiO2 NPs, followed by the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs, resulting in the deterioration of photocatalytic activities. However, the formation of nanocomposites with NGQDs leads to a narrow bandgap from 3.10 to 2.80 eV of rutile TiO2 followed by the bandgap alignment to allow photogenerated electrons to flow through the conduction band of TiO2 nanoparticles to NGQDs. Also, the narrow bandgap of NT–I nanocomposites leads to absorbing visible light and efficient dissociation of electron-hole pairs to be transferred to the conduction band of TiO2. The photogenerated electrons induce the reactive oxygen species by forming single oxygen, which causes the oxidative decomposition of ST. In Fig. 4, photoinduced electron-hole pairs induce the formation of reactive oxygen species to decompose of Safranin T. In the valence bands (VB), the reaction of holes with reactive OH– or H2O leads to the formation of
OH. In the conduction band (CB), photoinduced electrons lead to the formation of reactive oxygen radicals such as
O2 −,
OH, and H2O2. Subsequently, the hydroxyl radicals (
OH) originating from VB and CB reacts with Safranin T to be decomposed. The presence of oxygen in NT nanocomposites results in the stronger absorption of oxygen than that of bare rutile TiO2 [22]. Furthermore, the covalent bond between NGQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles by forming TiCO and TiON is believed to enhance electron transfer from the excited ST to TiO2 via NGQDs by allowing the excited electrons to be prevented from recombination, resulting in enhancement of light-driven photodegradation of ST. As shown in Fig. 3, photocatalytic performance is highly enhanced by 2.7 times due to the well-defined NGQD layers on TiO2 surfaces. Additionally, the relative thickness of NGQDs layers leads to the enhancement of the photodegradation of organic dyes as well as the hybridization between TiO2 and NGQDs.

In conclusion, the photoactive NGQD-TiO2 nanocomposites are synthesized in order to optimize the fabrication process to produce high-performance photocatalysts. The formation of well-defined deposition of NGQDs on a TiO2 surface was investigated by TEM, resulting in well-defined sub-3 nm NGQDs layers. NGQDs-TiO2 nanocomposites concealed to enhance visible light absorption by observing a red-shift of the band-edge and a substantial narrowing bandgap of the nanocomposites. We found that the NGQDs-TiO2 nanocomposites show the efficient photocatalytic performance in the visible light range at a decomposition rate of 5.00 × 10−3 min−1, which is increased by 2.7 times compared to that of bare rutile TiO2 nanoparticles. We believe that the fabrication strategy in this study will accelerate the practical uses of nanocomposites composed of semiconductor nanoparticles for producing highly photoactive hybrid materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by University of California, San Diego Heller Lab Royalty Fund BNGTTLB. The authors are grateful for Dr. Jennifer Wright for fruitful discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2018.10.058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
